# Transcrição: PLF_Spotlight_Jason

- **Fonte:** PLF_Spotlight_Jason
- **Duração:** 05:05
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Hi, I'm Jason Miles from Liberty Jane Clothing. A business was started together with my wife, Cinnamon, in February of 2008. When we started, we were living in Dixon, California. We had moved down from Seattle to Dixon. We had used this new mortgage product we had never heard of before in AMLone, and the mortgage broker was actually the son of one of my coworkers. And he had said, oh, you can refinance after a year. No big deal. So the year came, and we realized we couldn't refinance, and that the housing market had turned and we were stuck. So ultimately, our monthly mortgage went from a really high number to us, $2,700 a month to $4,400 a month, which was an insanely high number. And we had to go through a short sale process. We just couldn't keep it. So the little business we had started just wasn't strong enough to really salvage the situation. And I'll never forget, we were standing in the kitchen. It was January 2009. We were standing in the kitchen, and my middle daughter wrapped her arms around me and was crying and said, dad, you said we would never have to move again. And I was just crushed. To realize that I had personally moved us into a situation that sucked and that I couldn't get us out of without drama to the family, it was the low point. So in the summer of 2009, we had just moved back up to the Seattle area. And Cinnamon was done with the sewing and selling on eBay. She was just burned out. But there was this brand we had built, Liberty Jane clothing, and customers liked our stuff. And so over the course of about three to six months, we started to reimagine the business and consider what might be able to happen if we kind of changed our business model and did things differently. So that summer of 2009, we launched digitally downloadable patterns as a digital item. And we thought maybe that might be a way in which we could scale differently. And we put together this idea of doing something called a design academy. We tried it, and we had 20 students enroll. And a few months later, we did it again, and I think we had 22 students enroll. And I was on the hunt, trying to figure out how do I market this course. Is there some way I can launch this course and make it really scalable? And I started to just look what is out there in terms of launching a product, launching a new online course, and found product launch formula that summer. When I first saw the product launch formula strategy videos, it immediately clicked with me. And I remember thinking I'm not going to be able to talk Cinnamon into this.

## [03:00 - 05:05]

But I sat down with her. I said, look, this is the free videos. We watched them together. And I said, I think this will really work for the design academy. And so we decided to invest in the course, and we went for it. We launched the design academy again using the specific product launch formula methodology. And that next time, we had 225 students enroll. And it just felt like, I mean, a miracle. We didn't know there were that many people who are interested in our course and our topics. So in a three-year period, we went from 12,000 in total sales. That was our eBay year. And then we went to 36,000 with the digital goods and the design academy. And then the next year was over 100,000. And it's continued to scale from there. What started to happen was other doll clothes pattern makers said, hey, can you sell my doll clothes as well, my doll clothes patterns? And it turned into us selling for one or two at first. And then it had become more and more dozens started to ask us whether we could sell their digitally downloaded patterns or not. And ultimately, that turned into a marketplace. It has about 70 participants right now. We do 40 to 50,000 transactions a month. We've had 1.8 million patterns downloaded from that marketplace. That's called pixiefear.com. And our daughters even want to take over the business one day and run it. And they're ready for us to retire after they get through college. So when we bought the program initially and we really got into it and we had that first success, we could have never imagined what we would be able to do with the methodology of product launch. And we really tell people now, we've got two cornerstones of our business. Email marketing and product launch formula strategies. And we really bake that methodology into so many areas of our business. When we come out with new products, it's PLF strategy. When we're trying a new thing, I guess you could call it the launch echo, just continues to go on and on in interesting ways for us.
